regime_classification.2026-04-09:growth: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"growth","metric_summary":"GDP 7.8%; PMI 56.5","signal_text":"PMI composite at 56.5 flatters to deceive: manufacturing fell to 53.9 from 56.9 in a single month, and core sector output decelerated to 2.3% YoY from 4.7% — a 240bps miss that cement and steel dispatches confirm. GDP at 7.8% is real but decelerating from 8.4%, and the RBI's own forecast of 6.9% implies the statistical agency and the central bank are looking at different economies. Invert the bull case: if IIP capital goods at 12.5% is genuine investment acceleration, capacity utilisation should be rising — but the data is unavailable, which is itself a signal that the number may not flatter.","badge_label":"Expansion Mode","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:inflation: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"inflation","metric_summary":"CPI 3.21%; Fuel -3.78%%","signal_text":"Headline CPI at 3.21% masks a dangerous internal rotation: food inflation jumped to 3.47% from 2.13% in a single month while fuel deflation of -3.78% is doing the statistical heavy lifting. Core CPI at 3.4% is flat — which means the moment fuel stops deflating, headline CPI has a straight path to 4.5%+ with Brent at $98. RBI's own inflation forecast has already revised up to 4.6% from 4.2% — if they cut 50bps into this trajectory, the second-order effect is INR depreciation feeding imported food inflation back into CPI with a two-quarter lag.","badge_label":"Within Band","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:credit: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"credit","metric_summary":"Credit 13.8%; CD 83.04%","signal_text":"CD ratio at 83.04% — 81st percentile over a decade — with credit growing at 13.8% against deposit growth of 10.8%, a 300bps divergence that has widened for consecutive months. NBFC credit growth has already decelerated from 17% to 13% YoY, the first visible casualty of wholesale funding strain; corporate bond issuance at ₹1,12,000 crore confirms banks are rationing credit and borrowers are seeking alternatives. Last time India sustained a CD ratio above 80% during a rate-cut cycle — Q2 FY14 — the taper tantrum forced RBI to reverse with 75bps of emergency hikes within 12 months; today's FX reserves are $100bn higher, but the structural tension is identical.","badge_label":"Deposit Gap Stress","badge_type":"b-risk"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:policy: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"policy","metric_summary":"Repo 5.25%%","signal_text":"Repo at 5.25% with CPI at 3.21% implies a real rate of approximately 200bps — restrictive enough to justify a cut, but Brent at $98 and food inflation accelerating to 3.47% give the MPC legitimate cover to wait. Markets are pricing 50bps of cuts within two quarters, which would compress the India-US rate differential from 190bps to 140bps — at a moment when FII equity flows have swung ₹22,729 crore negative in a single month. Cutting into a widening CAD, a depreciating INR at the 84th percentile, and a CD ratio at its highest stress level since 2022 is a policy combination that requires a very high conviction on disinflation holding.","badge_label":"Rate Pause","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:capex: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"capex","metric_summary":"IIP CapG 12.5%%; CU Not available in search results%","signal_text":"IIP capital goods at 12.5% YoY — up sharply from 4.3% — is the most encouraging number in today's dataset, but core sector at 2.3% (cement, steel, electricity, coal) directly contradicts it: you cannot have genuine capex acceleration without core sector throughput. Real estate launches at 126,265 units — 0th percentile over a decade, an all-time low — despite an affordability index improving to 0.61 and home loan rates falling to 8% from 9.1%, which suggests developers are not convinced demand is durable enough to commit capital. Munger's inversion: if private capex were genuinely accelerating, GST collections on capex-intensive sectors would be outpacing the headline ₹2,00,640 crore — they are not.","badge_label":"Steady Investment","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:consumption: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"consumption","metric_summary":"GST 200640; PV 26.1%","signal_text":"GST at ₹2,00,640 crore crossing the ₹2 lakh crore threshold is structurally significant, but the Mishra question is whether this reflects genuine demand expansion or formalization reclassifying existing informal spend — two-wheeler sales at 28.68% YoY and CV sales at 28.89% suggest rural and freight demand is genuinely strong, not just a base effect. Passenger vehicle sales at 26.1% YoY confirm urban discretionary is holding, but airline passenger traffic at the 7th percentile and e-commerce GMV growth decelerating from 25% to 12.4% suggest the upper-middle consumer is pulling back. Retail mall vacancy rising to 5.7% from 5.1% while e-commerce decelerates simultaneously is the most underappreciated signal: neither physical nor digital retail is accelerating — consumption breadth is narrower than the headline implies.","badge_label":"Demand Surge","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-10:growth: {"run_date":"2026-04-10","dimension":"growth","metric_summary":"GDP 7.8%; PMI 56.5","signal_text":"PMI Composite at 56.5 flatters to deceive: Manufacturing PMI fell to 53.9 from 56.9, core sector decelerated to 2.3% from 4.7%, and airline passenger traffic dropped 9% to a 18-month low — none of which is consistent with a 7.8% economy firing on all cylinders. Invert the GDP print: the 170bps gap between the RBI's own forecast of 6.9% and the reported 7.8% demands a deflator explanation, not a celebration. What breaks this narrative is simple — if IIP capital goods momentum (up 12.5% this month but from a 4.3% prior base) doesn't sustain, the growth story reverts to its GVA skeleton, which is structurally closer to 6.5%.","badge_label":"Expansion Mode","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-10:inflation: {"run_date":"2026-04-10","dimension":"inflation","metric_summary":"CPI 3.21%; Fuel -3.78%%","signal_text":"Headline CPI at 3.21% is benign, but the composition is a slow-motion trap: food inflation has jumped to 3.47% from 2.13% in a single print, FAO Food Index is at 128.5 (a 2.5% MoM rise), and Brent at $96.44 means the fuel deflation buffer of -3.78% is arithmetically finite — one administered price revision ends it. Core CPI is sticky at 3.4%, unchanged for two consecutive months, which tells you demand-side pricing power hasn't broken despite the rate cycle. Munger's inversion: if RBI cuts into a food-inflation re-acceleration while Brent adds $15bn to the import bill for every $10 move, the second-order effect is a CPI overshoot toward 5% by Q3 FY27 — precisely when the rate cut transmission would be peaking.","badge_label":"Within Band","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-10:credit: {"run_date":"2026-04-10","dimension":"credit","metric_summary":"Credit 13.8%; CD 83.04%","signal_text":"CD ratio at 83.04% — the 81st percentile of its 10-year range — is the single most important number in today's dataset, and it is moving in the wrong direction from 82.5%. NBFC credit growth has already decelerated from 17% to 13% YoY, the classic early signal of wholesale funding stress; corporate bond issuance at ₹1.12 lakh crore sits at the 7th percentile — a decade low — meaning corporates are not accessing bond markets as a relief valve, they are rationing debt entirely. Historically, the last time India's CD ratio sustained above 80% during a rate-cut cycle was Q2 FY14; within four quarters, the taper tantrum forced 75bps of emergency RBI hikes — the setup today rhymes, except the external trigger this time is Brent, not Fed tapering.","badge_label":"Deposit Gap Stress","badge_type":"b-risk"}
regime_classification.2026-04-10:policy: {"run_date":"2026-04-10","dimension":"policy","metric_summary":"Repo 5.25%%","signal_text":"Repo at 5.25% with CPI at 3.21% implies a real policy rate of approximately +200bps — restrictive by India's historical standards, where neutral real rates have averaged 100-150bps. RBI's own inflation forecast has been revised up to 4.6% from 4.2%, which compresses the cutting room: a 25bps cut would still leave real rates above neutral, but the CD ratio at 83% means any rate cut that stimulates credit demand without a corresponding deposit response risks a macro-prudential response — higher risk weights on unsecured lending, as seen in November 2023. Home loan rates falling to 8% from 9.1% have already improved the affordability index to 0.61 from 0.46 — the transmission is working in housing; the question is whether the RBI can hold this line if food inflation prints above 4% in May.","badge_label":"Rate Pause","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-10:capex: {"run_date":"2026-04-10","dimension":"capex","metric_summary":"IIP CapG 12.5%%; CU Not available in search results%","signal_text":"IIP capital goods at +12.5% YoY looks strong until you note it follows a +4.3% prior print — the base effect is doing most of the work, not a genuine acceleration in investment orders. Core sector at 2.3% — covering steel, cement, electricity, and coal — is the most reliable capex proxy India has, and at an 18-month low, it contradicts the capital goods IIP entirely; cement dispatch and steel production don't lie about construction activity the way a single-month IIP print can. Capacity utilisation data is unavailable today, but auto and cement order book commentary points to plateauing utilisation — and Munger's question is the right one: if utilisation isn't above 75-80%, the incentive for private greenfield capex simply doesn't exist, regardless of what the budget allocates.","badge_label":"Steady Investment","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-10:consumption: {"run_date":"2026-04-10","dimension":"consumption","metric_summary":"GST 200640; PV 26.1%","signal_text":"GST collections at ₹2.006 lakh crore are a record, but Mishra's lens demands decomposition: at this level, a meaningful share of the YoY gain reflects formalization reclassification — informal-to-formal migration — rather than net new demand creation, particularly as e-commerce GMV growth has halved from 25% to 12.4% YoY, the 15th percentile of its range. Vehicle sales tell a bifurcated story: PV at +26.1%, two-wheelers at +28.68%, and CVs at +28.89% all signal broad-based demand — but airline passenger traffic at 35.08 lakh, down 9% and at a 7th-percentile low, suggests urban discretionary spending is softening at the margin. Invert the GST record: if consumption were genuinely broad-based, e-commerce GMV and airline traffic — the two most real-time consumption proxies — would not both be printing multi-year lows simultaneously.","badge_label":"Demand Surge","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-11:growth: {"run_date":"2026-04-11","dimension":"growth","metric_summary":"GDP 7.8%; PMI 56.5","signal_text":"GDP at 7.8% looks unimpeachable until you decompose it: PMI Composite fell to 56.5 from 58.9, PMI Manufacturing dropped to 53.9 from 56.9, and core sector growth collapsed to 2.3% YoY from 4.7% — the sharpest single-month deceleration in 18 months. IIP capital goods at 12.5% is a base-effect mirage, not organic acceleration; the prior month was 4.3%, and without capacity utilisation data to anchor it, the number is a headline without a foundation. Invert the bull case: if PMI sub-indices are rolling over while GDP is still printing 7.8%, the statistical machinery is running ahead of the real economy — and the RBI's own forecast of 6.9% is a quiet admission that the gap closes downward, not upward.","badge_label":"Expansion Mode","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-11:inflation: {"run_date":"2026-04-11","dimension":"inflation","metric_summary":"CPI 3.21%; Fuel -3.78%%","signal_text":"CPI at 3.21% is benign in aggregate, but the composition is shifting uncomfortably: food inflation jumped to 3.47% from 2.13% in a single month, while core CPI holds at 3.4% — meaning the headline is being held down entirely by fuel deflation at -3.78%, a subsidy-and-crude-price gift that reverses the moment Brent stabilises above $95. WPI at 2.13% and rising, FAO food index at 128.5 (up from 125.3), and copper at a 10-year high of $5.868/lb are the upstream signals that CPI is borrowing tranquility from a fuel line that won't stay negative. If RBI cuts into a food-inflation uptick, the second-order effect is INR carry unwind → imported inflation feedback → CPI back above 4.5% within two quarters.","badge_label":"Within Band","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-11:credit: {"run_date":"2026-04-11","dimension":"credit","metric_summary":"Credit 13.8%; CD 83.04%","signal_text":"CD ratio at 83.04% — the 81st percentile of the past decade — with credit growing at 13.8% YoY against deposit growth of only 10.8%, a 300bps divergence widening for three consecutive months. NBFC credit growth has already decelerated from 17% to 13% YoY, the canary in the wholesale funding mine; corporate bond issuance at ₹1.12 lakh crore confirms that large borrowers are bypassing banks entirely — which means the residual bank credit is increasingly concentrated in retail and SME, precisely the segments with the least collateral buffer. Last time India's CD ratio sustained above 80% while the system was simultaneously absorbing NBFC stress was Q3 FY19 — within six months, IL&FS triggered a liquidity seizure that took 200bps of NBFC spreads wider and forced RBI into emergency OMOs; the setup today has better FX reserves but the same structural imbalance.","badge_label":"Deposit Gap Stress","badge_type":"b-risk"}
regime_classification.2026-04-11:policy: {"run_date":"2026-04-11","dimension":"policy","metric_summary":"Repo 5.25%%","signal_text":"Repo at 5.25% with CPI at 3.21% implies a real rate of 204bps — restrictive by India's historical standards, where neutral real rates sit around 100-150bps. RBI's inflation forecast has been revised up to 4.6% from 4.2%, which arithmetically compresses the case for cuts even as the growth narrative invites them — the committee is caught between a headline that permits easing and an upstream commodity complex that punishes it. For housing: the average home loan rate has already fallen to 8.0% from 9.1%, affordability index improved to 0.61 from 0.46 — yet real estate sales are at an all-time low of 101,675 units against all-time high launches of 126,265 units, suggesting rate transmission to demand is broken, not lagged.","badge_label":"Rate Pause","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-11:capex: {"run_date":"2026-04-11","dimension":"capex","metric_summary":"IIP CapG 12.5%%; CU Not available in search results%","signal_text":"IIP capital goods at 12.5% is the number that will be cited in every bull deck today — and it is the number most likely to mislead. Core sector at 2.3% (cement, steel, electricity, coal combined) is the physical proxy for actual investment activity, and it is decelerating sharply from 4.7%; you cannot have genuine capex acceleration when the materials that build factories are growing at a 10-quarter low. Capacity utilisation data is unavailable — and RBI's deliberate silence on this metric, given that sub-70% utilisation historically precedes private capex stagnation, is itself a signal. Munger's inversion: if private capex were genuinely accelerating, cement dispatches and steel consumption would confirm it — they do not.","badge_label":"Steady Investment","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-11:consumption: {"run_date":"2026-04-11","dimension":"consumption","metric_summary":"GST 200640; PV 26.1%","signal_text":"GST collections at ₹2.01 lakh crore in the month — the 84th percentile — are the single most credible consumption signal in India's data architecture, because they cannot be revised away or deflated by a convenient price index. Vehicle sales corroborate: PV at +26.1% YoY, two-wheelers at +28.68% YoY, commercial vehicles at +28.89% YoY — a broad-based surge that, unlike PMI, is a hard transaction count. But invert: airline passenger volumes fell to 35.08 mn from 38.59 mn, e-commerce GMV growth collapsed to 12.4% from 25%, and retail mall vacancy is rising to 5.7% — the urban discretionary consumer who shops online and flies is softening, while the semi-urban buyer of a two-wheeler is surging; this is a consumption rotation, not a consumption broadening, and it has different implications for margin profiles across the consumption universe.","badge_label":"Demand Surge","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-12:growth: {"run_date":"2026-04-12","dimension":"growth","metric_summary":"GDP 7.8%; PMI 56.5","signal_text":"GDP at 7.8% is the headline; the quality test is in the decomposition. PMI Composite fell to 56.5 from 58.9, Manufacturing slid to 53.9 from 56.9, and core sector growth collapsed to 2.3% from 4.7% — a 240bps miss that no press release will mention. IIP capital goods at 12.5% looks like acceleration until you note it is bouncing off a 4.3% base and capacity utilisation data is unavailable — the number flatters to deceive. Invert the bull case: if this growth is consumption-led (GST strong, PV sales up 26%) but investment-led indicators are softening, the next quarter's GDP will depend on whether the consumer keeps spending — and affordability data suggests she is being stretched.","badge_label":"Expansion Mode","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-12:inflation: {"run_date":"2026-04-12","dimension":"inflation","metric_summary":"CPI 3.21%; Fuel -3.78%%","signal_text":"CPI at 3.21% is within the RBI's band, but the direction matters more than the level — food inflation jumped to 3.47% from 2.13% in a single month, and the FAO food index is at 128.5, up from 125.3. Core CPI is flat at 3.4%, which is the number the RBI actually watches — and at 3.4% with a repo rate of 5.25%, the real policy rate is a restrictive 185bps. The Munger inversion: if the RBI cuts while food inflation is re-accelerating, the second-order effect is a weaker INR feeding imported inflation back into CPI with a two-quarter lag — the very outcome the cut was meant to avoid.","badge_label":"Within Band","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-12:credit: {"run_date":"2026-04-12","dimension":"credit","metric_summary":"Credit 13.8%; CD 83.04%","signal_text":"CD ratio at 83.04% — 81st percentile, highest in this cycle — with credit growing at 13.8% against deposits at 10.8%, a 300bps divergence widening for three consecutive months. NBFC credit growth has decelerated sharply to 13% from 17%, the tell that wholesale funding stress is already transmitting. Corporate bond issuance at ₹112,000 cr sits at the 7th percentile — the lowest in a decade — meaning banks are rationing credit and forcing corporates to capital markets at precisely the moment those markets are pricing in rate cuts that may not arrive. The last time India's CD ratio sustained above 80% while the repo rate was being cut was Q2 FY14; within 12 months, the taper tantrum forced RBI to reverse with 75bps of emergency hikes — today's FX reserves are $100bn higher, but the structural tension rhymes.","badge_label":"Deposit Gap Stress","badge_type":"b-risk"}
regime_classification.2026-04-12:policy: {"run_date":"2026-04-12","dimension":"policy","metric_summary":"Repo 5.25%%","signal_text":"Repo at 5.25% with CPI at 3.21% implies a real rate of 204bps — restrictive by any measure, yet the RBI's own inflation forecast has been revised up to 4.6% from 4.2%, which compresses the forward real rate and narrows the cutting window. At 8% average home loan rates (down from 9.1%), housing affordability is at its worst-ever reading of 0.61 — rate cuts have not cleared the affordability problem because prices have risen faster than the rate relief. For corporate capex, a 5.25% repo with a CD ratio at 83% means the marginal cost of bank funding is rising even as the policy rate holds — the transmission is broken in the direction that matters.","badge_label":"Rate Pause","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-12:capex: {"run_date":"2026-04-12","dimension":"capex","metric_summary":"IIP CapG 12.5%%; CU Not available in search results%","signal_text":"IIP capital goods at 12.5% YoY is the number the bulls will cite; core sector at 2.3% — down from 4.7% — is the number that matters. Core sector covers eight infrastructure industries (steel, cement, electricity, coal, crude, refinery, fertiliser, natural gas) that account for 40% of IIP weight; when core sector and capital goods diverge by this magnitude, the capital goods print is likely driven by defence or electronics imports, not domestic industrial investment. PMI Manufacturing new orders are weakening (53.9 from 56.9), and without capacity utilisation data, the honest answer is: we cannot confirm whether this capex is expansion or replacement. Munger's test — if private capex were genuinely accelerating, cement dispatches and steel consumption would confirm it; they are not.","badge_label":"Steady Investment","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-12:consumption: {"run_date":"2026-04-12","dimension":"consumption","metric_summary":"GST 200640; PV 26.1%","signal_text":"GST at ₹2,00,640 cr is a record, but Mishra's lens demands decomposition: April advance filings and the base effect of formalisation reclassifying informal spend can explain a significant portion of any GST beat — this is not purely demand. Vehicle sales are unambiguously strong — PV up 26.1%, two-wheelers up 28.68%, commercial vehicles up 28.89% — but airline passenger traffic fell to the 7th percentile at 35.08 mn, down from 38.59, and e-commerce GMV growth collapsed to 12.4% from 25%. The divergence between physical goods (strong) and discretionary services (weakening) suggests the consumption surge is concentrated in the mass-market durable segment — not the broad-based demand the headline implies.","badge_label":"Demand Surge","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-13:growth: {"run_date":"2026-04-13","dimension":"growth","metric_summary":"GDP 7.8%; PMI 56.5","signal_text":"India's headline GDP at 7.8% flatters on first read — but PMI Composite has slipped from 58.9 to 56.5 in a single month, manufacturing PMI dropped 300bps to 53.9, and core sector growth halved to 2.3% from 4.7%. regime_classification.2026-04-19:consumption: {"run_date":"2026-04-19","dimension":"consumption","metric_summary":"GST 200640; PV 26.1%","signal_text":"GST at ₹2,00,640 cr is a record, but Mishra's lens demands decomposition: how much is genuine demand versus formalisation reclassifying existing spend, and how much is April advance filings inflating the month? regime_classification.2026-04-20:growth: {"run_date":"2026-04-20","dimension":"growth","metric_summary":"GDP 7.8%; PMI 56.5","signal_text":"GDP at 7.8% is the headline; the inversion is that PMI Composite fell to 56.5 from 58.9, manufacturing slid to 53.9 from 56.9, and core sector growth collapsed to 2.3% from 4.7% — the slowest in 18 months. regime_classification.2026-04-20:policy: {"run_date":"2026-04-20","dimension":"policy","metric_summary":"Repo 5.25%%","signal_text":"Repo at 5.25%, unchanged, with RBI's own inflation forecast at 4.6% — the real rate is approximately 105bps positive, which is neither accommodative nor restrictive enough to resolve the CD ratio stress. regime_classification.2026-05-01:credit: {"run_date":"2026-05-01","dimension":"credit","metric_summary":"Credit 13.8%; CD 83.04%","signal_text":"CD ratio at 83.04% — the 81st percentile of the last decade — with credit growing at 13.8% against deposit growth of 10.8%, a 300bps divergence widening for three consecutive months. regime_classification.2026-05-01:policy: {"run_date":"2026-05-01","dimension":"policy","metric_summary":"Repo 5.25%%","signal_text":"Repo at 5.25% with CPI at 3.21% implies a real rate of approximately 200bps — restrictive by any historical Indian standard, yet the RBI's own GDP forecast of 6.9% sits 90bps below the actual print, suggesting the MPC is not convinced the growth is durable. regime_classification.2026-05-01:consumption: {"run_date":"2026-05-01","dimension":"consumption","metric_summary":"GST 200640; PV 26.1%","signal_text":"GST at ₹2,00,640 crore and PV sales up 26.1% YoY look like a demand surge — but Sig7 is the honest read. regime_classification.2026-05-02:growth: {"run_date":"2026-05-02","dimension":"growth","metric_summary":"GDP 7.8%; PMI 56.5","signal_text":"GDP at 7.8% is the headline; the quality test fails on three proxies. executive_summary.2026-04-08:1:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":1,"para_label":"India Macro Regime","para_html":"

GDP 7.8% with PMI Composite at 57 puts India in unambiguous expansion — 3 of 6 macro dimensions are in growth mode simultaneously. GST collections at ₹2,00,064 Cr YoY alongside PV sales growth of 10.5% confirm demand is broad-based, not sector-specific. CPI at 3.21% with fuel deflation at -3.78% gives RBI room to hold the repo at 5.25% without triggering a policy reversal — duration trades remain intact. Credit growth at 14.3% against deposit growth of 10.9% — CD ratio now 83.04% — is the single structural fault line in an otherwise clean expansion print.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-08:2:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":2,"para_label":"Global Macro Regime","para_html":"

S&P 500 at 6,779 (+2.5%) and Nasdaq at 22,653 (+3.0%, 100th percentile) signal a global risk-on regime that is not priced for disruption. Brent at $94.98/bbl — down 13% from $109.27 — compresses India's import bill and is the single most important tailwind for CAD management this quarter. Copper at $5.766/lb (95th percentile, 10-year range) and iron ore surging to $161.91/t from $108.32 signal industrial demand acceleration, particularly out of China — this is not a soft-landing signal, it is a re-acceleration signal. DXY at 98.84 and US 10Y at 4.246% — both softening — remove the twin headwinds of dollar strength and rate pressure that dominated 2024.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-08:3:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":3,"para_label":"Liquidity Conditions","para_html":"

SIP AUM at ₹16.64 Tr on 9.92 Cr accounts is structural bedrock — a -3.7% MoM dip in SIP inflows to ₹29,845 Cr is noise against 15% YoY growth. Equity MF net inflows accelerated to ₹25,978 Cr (prev: ₹24,029 Cr), and NFO collections exploded to ₹3,955 Cr from ₹806 Cr — DII buying is not slowing, it is rotating into new paper. India VIX collapsing 20.25% to 19.70 from 24.7 — concurrent with US VIX falling to 21.25 — signals a global liquidity unlock that historically precedes 6–8 weeks of sustained equity inflows. FII positioning remains the swing variable; INR stability at 92.59 against a weakening DXY (98.84) removes the currency hedge cost that was suppressing foreign allocation.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-08:4:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":4,"para_label":"Equity + Real Estate Implications","para_html":"

Nifty50 at 23,997 (+3.78%) and Sensex at 77,563 (+3.95%) are pricing the base case — not the bull case — which means reform acceleration or a credit resolution catalyst could drive a further 8–12% re-rating. Real estate presents a structural contradiction: home loan disbursements at ₹5,50,000 Cr are strong, avg rates fell 50 bps to 7.65%, yet RE sales declined 6.7% to 1,01,675 units against an affordability index deteriorating 32% to 0.61 — volume is being bought by rate cuts, not by income growth. Office REITs are the cleaner play: absorption at 83.3 (+13.7%) with vacancy compressing to 15.2% — Embassy REIT at ₹429.85 and Mindspace at ₹449.98 are at 75–79th percentiles, not stretched. Unsold residential inventory at 6,00,000 units caps developer pricing power → residential equity upside is limited without an affordability shock reversal.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-08:5:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":5,"para_label":"Key Risks to Monitor","para_html":"

Ranked by probability × impact: #1 — CD ratio at 83.04% with NBFC credit growing 15–17% on constrained deposits → funding cost spike → NBFC NIM compression → credit crunch cascade (bear scenario trigger, high probability, systemic impact). #2 — Affordability index at 0.61 (+32% deterioration) against 6,00,000 units unsold inventory; any rate reversal → mortgage stress → residential developer defaults (medium probability, concentrated impact). #3 — Copper at 95th percentile and iron ore at $161.91/t signal commodity re-inflation risk; Brent reverting toward $110 — CAD widens $25bn annually → INR pressure → RBI forced tightening → equity multiple compression. Watch the CD ratio print for April — at 83.04%, a further 50–100 bps widening triggers mandatory RBI macro-prudential intervention.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:1:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":1,"para_label":"India Macro Regime","para_html":"

7.8% GDP growth with PMI composite at 56.5 is the headline; the subtext is a growth regime that is decelerating on every sub-indicator that matters — manufacturing PMI fell 300bps in a month to 53.9, core sector output dropped to 2.3% from 4.7%, and the RBI's own forecast of 6.9% sits 90bps below the statistical agency's print. GST at ₹2,00,640 crore — the first ₹2 lakh crore month — is real, but two-wheeler sales at 28.68% and CV sales at 28.89% YoY are doing more analytical work than the GST headline: rural and freight demand is genuinely recovering, not just being formalized. IIP capital goods at 12.5% would be unambiguously positive if core sector and cement dispatches corroborated it — they do not, which makes this a data point to verify, not celebrate. India's growth is real but narrowing: the formal economy is expanding, the informal economy is stabilizing, and the capex cycle is more promise than proof.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:2:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":2,"para_label":"Global Macro Regime","para_html":"

US GDP SAAR at 0.7% — down from 4.4%, a 370bps deceleration in a single quarter — is the most consequential global data point this week, yet S&P 500 at the 96th percentile and Nasdaq at an all-time high suggest equity markets have decided this is a soft patch, not a stall. US core PCE at 3.1%, ticking up from 3.0%, with Fed funds at 3.625% and the balance sheet expanding to $6,675bn, means the Fed is simultaneously easing liquidity while inflation re-accelerates — a combination that historically resolves with either a policy mistake or a growth shock. Brent at $98.06 and WTI at $99.67 — with iron ore surging 50% in a month to $161.91/tonne — suggest commodity markets are pricing a China demand revival that China's own GDP at 4.5% (down from 5%) does not yet justify. Global PMI composite at 51 — barely above contraction — with the US at 50.3 and Europe at sub-1% GDP growth means India's 7.8% is an outlier that will attract capital only if the INR holds.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:3:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":3,"para_label":"Liquidity Conditions","para_html":"

FII equity net flows swung ₹22,729 crore negative in a single month — from +₹22,615 crore to -₹114 crore — while DII support at +₹128 crore is at the 22nd percentile, meaning domestic institutions are not absorbing the FII exit with conviction. SIP inflows at ₹29,845 crore are declining from ₹31,002 crore, and SIP YoY growth has decelerated from 17% to 15% — the retail liquidity engine that cushioned every FII exit since 2022 is running at lower pressure. CD ratio at 83.04% — with credit at 13.8% and deposits at 10.8% — means the banking system's internal liquidity is being consumed faster than it is being replenished, forcing corporate bond issuance to ₹1,12,000 crore as a pressure valve. If RBI cuts 50bps as markets expect, the India-US rate differential compresses to 140bps at the precise moment FII outflows are accelerating — the INR at the 84th percentile has no margin for error.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:4:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":4,"para_label":"Equity + Real Estate Implications","para_html":"

Nifty at 23,775 — 76th percentile — with Bank Nifty at the 90th percentile and India VIX rising to 20.4 from 19.7 creates a specific risk: banks are the most expensive segment of a market facing the most acute structural stress — CD ratio at 83%, NBFC deceleration, and potential macro-prudential tightening on unsecured lending. Real estate presents the sharpest internal contradiction in today's dataset: affordability at a decade-best 0.61, home loan rates at 8% (down from 9.1%), and ₹5,50,000 crore in disbursements — yet launches at an all-time low 0th percentile and sales declining to 1,01,675 units from 1,08,970, with unsold inventory at 6,00,000 units. Office absorption at 29.9 mn sqft with vacancy compressing 330bps to 13.9% is the one clean positive — Bengaluru rents up 8.4% to ₹102/sqft — but Nifty IT flat YoY means the equity market is not pricing the physical demand that office REITs are experiencing. Embassy REIT at ₹437 and Mindspace at ₹450 — both near 79th percentile — are the better expression of the office thesis than IT equities.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:5:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":5,"para_label":"Key Risks to Monitor","para_html":"

Ranked by probability × impact: first, Brent sustaining above $98 — every $10 adds ~$15bn to India's import bill — CAD widens 40bps — INR depreciates 2-3% — imported food inflation feeds CPI with a two-quarter lag, directly threatening the rate-cut narrative and the INR stability that FII re-entry requires. Second, CD ratio breaching 84% — the level at which RBI has historically intervened with macro-prudential measures on risk weights — triggering NBFC funding costs to spike, credit growth to decelerate sharply, and consumption-linked equities to re-rate lower. Third, US core PCE at 3.1% and rising with GDP at 0.7% — stagflation is not the base case but it is now a 25% probability scenario — DXY strengthening from 98.9 would accelerate FII outflows from EM including India at the worst possible moment. Fourth, iron ore at $161.91 — a 50% monthly surge — if sustained, steel input costs rise, infrastructure project IRRs compress, and the capex cycle that IIP capital goods at 12.5% is supposed to signal gets deferred. Watch Friday's RBI liquidity data and weekly deposit growth figures — if the credit-deposit gap widens beyond 300bps for a third consecutive month, the bear scenario probability doubles.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-10:1:
{"run_date":"2026-04-10","para_num":1,"para_label":"India Macro Regime","para_html":"

7.8% GDP sits 90bps above the RBI's own forecast of 6.9% — a gap that demands a deflator audit, not a growth upgrade. PMI Composite at 56.5 is decelerating from 58.9, manufacturing sub-index has dropped 300bps to 53.9, and core sector at 2.3% — an 18-month low — is the ground-level rebuttal to the headline.
